summary: I work at Pingtung University in Taiwan, where my research field is mathematics education. Recently, I have been focusing on indigenous mathematics curriculum development. My role there inspired me to learn more about similar work being done elsewhere, especially in Alaska.

Taiwan is my home country, where I live and work at Pingtung University. The weather and lifestyle there are very different from what I experienced during my visit to Alaska. Leaving Taiwan in July 2017 was a mix of nervousness and excitement for me.

I visited Alaska in 2017 for six months to study indigenous mathematics curriculum development. I was impressed by the natural beauty there, enjoying glacier cruises, hiking, snow, and skiing with my family. Alaska's environment and culture gave me a unique perspective that I hope to revisit someday.

I applied for a grant to visit the University of Alaska Anchorage (UAA) to learn more about their indigenous mathematics curriculum research. During my stay, I was able to observe how teachers implement the curriculum and gain valuable insights for my own research. This visit helped me build connections with UAA and broaden my academic perspective.

I stayed in Anchorage during my six-month visit to Alaska in 2017. Life there was very different from Taiwan; I had more time to think and reflect without my usual teaching duties or family around. Anchorage was also my base to explore local schools and experience the culture firsthand.

While in Anchorage, I had the opportunity to experience the Native Culture Charter School, which gave me a new perspective on mathematics curriculum and teaching for native people. Observing their school system was an important part of my visit and contributed significantly to my research on indigenous education.
